points: A rational number can be written in the form p/q (where p and q are integers and q is not equal to zero), whereas an irrational number cannot be written in this form.
The collection of all rational and irrational numbers together forms the collection of real numbers, where every real number represents a unique point on the real number line.
The decimal expansion of a rational number is either terminating or non-terminating recurring.
The decimal expansion of an irrational number is non-terminating non-recurring.
The sum, difference, product, or quotient of a non-zero rational number and an irrational number is always irrational.
To rationalise a denominator containing a square root, multiply both the numerator and the denominator by an appropriate conjugate expression.
For a real number a > 0 and rational numbers p and q, the laws of exponents hold: (a^p)(a^q) = a^(p+q), (a^p)^q = a^(pq), (a^p)/(a^q) = a^(p-q), and (a^p)(b^p) = (ab)^p.
